unique_issues.summary: Label construction uses analyst escalation or incident ticket linkage as the positive class, so the evaluation measures analyst workflow decisions rather than verified true intrusions.
Ticket-history features may leak label information because they are derived from prior IR/ticket activity on the same entity, so they can act as a proxy for the positive label or incident membership.
The static entity store may have been populated retroactively or built from the full historical period, which could leak future entity attributes or topology into training-time features.
Using nDCG@10 for hyperparameter selection may be unreliable in sparse-label settings or when query construction is unspecified.
nDCG@10 can misrepresent operational ranking quality when alert volume varies across shifts or days, because it evaluates a fixed cutoff on different queue fractions.
The 24-hour escalation window may introduce time-of-day bias if analyst shift timing affects whether alerts become positive labels.
Ticket-history features leak label information because they reflect analyst escalation / incident ticket linkage and encode entity-level positive rates, which will not generalize to novel campaigns.
Excluding alerts without disposition within 30 days biases the dataset toward higher-positive-rate, operationally unrepresentative cases.
Graph/entity features may leak future information if the entity store is not time-indexed per alert and includes relationships from later alerts.
The ablation design is incomplete because it cannot separate the contribution of entity-graph features from ticket-history features.
Reporting a single aggregate nDCG@10 over a long, non-stationary test window can conflate ranking quality with temporal drift.
Hyperparameter tuning on a short validation window may not align with performance over a longer, non-stationary test horizon.
The query construction for nDCG@10 is underspecified, so varying queue depths may make the metric operationally inconsistent across shifts.
Random stratified session-level split across 18 months causes temporal leakage / future sessions in training and evaluation, inflating performance under temporal drift and seasonality.
The comparison is confounded because it contrasts GBM with logistic regression while also changing the feature set; a missing baseline is GBM trained on aggregate features only.
Product ID encoding for the GBM may leak target information or conflate catalog identity with sequence signal, making the source of any gain ambiguous.
Sequence truncation to 50 events is underspecified (head vs. tail) and may discard important late-session behavior, affecting the comparison with aggregate features.
Raw product ID features may look strong under a random split but degrade under temporal deployment because of catalog churn and seasonality, so the apparent advantage may be split-specific.

The model comparison is unfair because the proposed model uses both a more expressive model class and richer features than the baseline, so improvement cannot be attributed to sequence features alone.
Using the test set as a tiebreaker for model selection when validation scores are tied biases the reported test performance upward.
Full-corpus preprocessing/normalization was fit before the temporal split, leaking future test-period information into training and validation.

The transformer-baseline comparison is confounded because the transformer gets host identifiers while the baseline does not, so the main result is ambiguous between sequence modeling and identifier access.
Identity-removal ablation is not a clean test because hyperparameters were tuned on the identifier-inclusive model and/or the remaining proxy features still leak host identity.
Temporal cross-validation folds are too short and misaligned with campaign timescales, making pAUC-based hyperparameter selection noisy and potentially unstable.
Bootstrap confidence intervals are anticonservative because session labels are temporally/campaign clustered and not i.i.d.
The 24-hour labeling/confirmation window introduces asymmetric or directional label noise that can bias evaluation.
Analyst-confirmed labels may introduce survivorship bias toward detectable attacks and exclude more evasive APT behavior.
The source-host alert count is a proxy for host identity that survives the identifier ablation, so the ablation does not fully remove identity information.
The metric pAUC-ROC is misaligned with the operational goal of minimizing alert fatigue / false positives under analyst capacity constraints.

Using claim-line count as the target instead of high allowed amounts changes the problem from cost prediction to utilization-volume prediction, so the stated hypothesis about cost drivers is not actually tested.

The label is a within-period rank/top-decile outcome based on follow-up claim-line counts, making it vulnerable to cohort composition drift across temporal splits and only indirectly related to the intended clinical construct.
The transformer-vs-GBDT comparison is confounded because the models may differ in feature access/representation, and the GBDT baseline lacks a bag-of-codes version to isolate architecture effects.

The transformer hypothesis is not cleanly isolated because there is no logistic regression baseline to determine whether engineered features already explain the performance gains, and rare-code UNK behavior may hurt subpopulations.
The randomized-order ablation cannot separate temporal-ordering effects from order-independent co-occurrence effects, so the three-way comparison is hard to interpret.
Using precision at 10% recall for hyperparameter selection may overfit model calibration on the validation split under temporal shift.
Re-training the transformer on combined train+validation data changes the effective vocabulary and makes the selected architecture inconsistent with the test-time model.
The GBDT baseline omits clinically standard features such as HCC scores and validated chronic condition flags, so it is not a strong proxy for current practice.
The 18-month single-payer time span induces vocabulary and coding drift that can disproportionately affect transformer embeddings for rare or emergent codes.
Precision at 10% recall may favor models that concentrate on easy positives, and the comparison lacks a trivial prior-utilization baseline.
Click labels derived from BM25 production rankings confound the learned model with the teacher ranker rather than an independent relevance signal.
The stratified label budget is too small per query-type/doc-age/month cell to support reliable claims about where gains are concentrated.
Majority-vote resolution of high-disagreement annotation pairs may leave noisy ambiguous judgments treated as equally certain in evaluation.
Frozen clinical BERT embeddings may be mismatched to the portal's document language and domain, making the semantic similarity feature noisy or uninformative.
Including impression rank as a feature does not debias click data; it can encode position bias instead.
Historical CTR features may leak relevance information across train/test if the split is not by exact query string.
Repeated hyperparameter tuning on a small validation set can overfit to validation noise and inflate reported test performance.
Bootstrap confidence intervals computed over query-document pairs instead of queries underestimate variance because pairs within a query are correlated.
Historical CTR plus impression rank may still leave residual position/popularity bias, so apparent gains could reflect popularity rather than relevance.
The ranker's larger hyperparameter search space relative to the fixed validation budget increases the chance of overfitting compared with BM25.
The model should check whether frozen clinical BERT captures institution-specific vocabulary before relying on it as a core similarity feature.
The stratified query-level split mixes calendar periods across train and test, allowing future document states and popularity patterns to leak into evaluation.
Using Precision@10 with binary relevance discards graded labels and is misaligned with early-rank semantic relevance objectives that NDCG@10 would capture better.
Repeated customer observations can leak the same individual across train/test splits, inflating performance via identity memorization and near-duplicate sequences.

The 90-day prior history requirement excludes or truncates genuinely newly acquired users, so the 'recently acquired' subgroup is not representative of the target population and the subgroup claim is not testable as stated.
Marginal calibration metrics like ECE may miss subgroup-specific miscalibration, such as poor calibration for short sequences.
The sequence model has more tunable hyperparameters than XGBoost, increasing the risk of overfitting the validation window's seasonal structure during model selection.

Class weighting is implemented differently across the neural sequence model and the sklearn/XGBoost baselines, which can silently alter thresholds, operating-point metrics, and calibration comparisons.
The architecture is underspecified (LSTM vs. Transformer), and without an order-permutation ablation it is unclear whether a null result reflects lack of sequence signal or architecture misfit.
The 'recently acquired ≤30 days' cohort is logically empty under the stated 90-day prior history requirement, making that stratified analysis untestable as written.
Identical class weighting across model families may create asymmetric optimization dynamics that destabilize sequence-model training more than tree-based baselines.
Learned event-type embeddings may be brittle to covariate shift in event-frequency distributions across train/validation/test windows, while count-based tabular aggregates are more robust.
Rolling 24-hour contact-intensity feature may leak label information by incorporating post-open support touches/routing/escalation activity tied to the escalation outcome.
Filtering out tickets with inter-rater agreement below 80% removes ambiguous hard cases and makes the train/evaluation distribution easier than production.
The baseline comparison is unfair because it withholds additional structured/metadata/history features from the baseline, so gains cannot be attributed specifically to historical encoding.
Macro-F1 threshold tuning on a seasonally imbalanced validation set may not transfer to the test window if prevalence shifts.
TF-IDF vocabulary and document-frequency statistics were fit globally on the full 24-month corpus, leaking future terminology into the baseline and making the comparison with the transformer unfair.

The reported 'top-1 macro-averaged accuracy' / 'macro-averaged accuracy' metric is ambiguous or underspecified and may not align with the operational goal of reducing misrouted tickets or protecting rare classes.

The thread-history ablation does not cleanly separate the effect of context access from architecture/modeling choices because the baseline comparison with thread text and intermediate controls are unspecified.
Using the validation set for both hyperparameter tuning and drift detection without pre-registration risks biasing model selection and conflating two distinct evaluation procedures.
The 3-month validation window may be too short and noisy to support reliable drift detection and early stopping.
The no-thread-history ablation cannot distinguish learned text representations from subword-tokenization/OOV robustness because those effects are confounded with the transformer architecture.
Macro-averaged accuracy may be unstable under label drift because deprecated or newly introduced categories can disproportionately affect the metric when class support changes.
Primary metric ECE is misaligned with the stated precision-recall / outreach objective, so the experiment may not answer its own hypothesis.
Random enrollment-level splitting mixes historical and future learners, causing temporal leakage and an optimistic evaluation relative to deployment on a future cohort.
Standard equal-width-bin ECE may underweight the high-risk tail under class imbalance, making it a poor operational signal for intervention decisions.
Learner-level splitting may still allow course-run-level leakage via memorization of course-specific token patterns appearing in both train and test.
Absence of post-hoc calibration disadvantages the sequence model on ECE in a way that could have been corrected.
Temporal distribution shift in course-run characteristics across cohorts may differentially hurt the sequence model relative to the baseline.
Hyperparameter search is asymmetric, giving the sequence model more validation-set tuning opportunities than the logistic regression baseline.
Non-chronological stratified splitting can leak future behavioral patterns and campaign artifacts into training, inflating performance.
Using all events within 72 hours of incident/ATO confirmation as positives contaminates labels with post-detection incident-response activity and other non-attacker artifacts.
The label set derived from IR records suffers detection bias, because only incidents already surfaced by existing monitoring are labeled positive.
Global label-shuffle or future-incident negative controls do not rule out user-identity proxy/confounding.
Tuning both the ML model and the rules baseline on the same validation set is asymmetric because the ML model has far greater capacity, which can inflate the apparent gap.
A logistic regression baseline is needed to separate the benefit of nonlinearity from the benefit of feature engineering.
Per-user deviation z-scores assume stationarity within the training window and can bias features for new hires, role changes, or travel-heavy users.
Using the test set to choose the operating threshold and alert budget contaminates evaluation.
Random partitioning across all terms causes temporal leakage by training on future-term records relative to held-out terms, so prospective generalization is overstated.
Course-level dropout rate computed from pooled training data across terms leaks label information from the same course into validation/test rows.
Different feature sets have different dimensionality/optimization difficulty, so equal tuning budget does not ensure a fair comparison of model quality.
Using held-out test results to resolve validation/test reversals or select the final configuration contaminates the test set and biases generalization estimates.
The Recall @ FPR ≤ 0.15 metric is underspecified or high-variance without a clear thresholding procedure and confidence intervals.
Random cross-sectional splitting can place the same student in both train and test, causing temporal leakage via repeated exposure and prior-history features.
Feature Set A contains Feature Set B plus additional information, so any advantage is confounded by larger feature space rather than temporal structure alone.
Same-term course-section effects and repeated student records can appear in both train and held-out splits under random stratified splitting, inflating apparent performance.
Feature Set A and Feature Set B are not informationally equivalent, so the comparison does not isolate the value of temporal order without an ablation.
Random stratified partitioning across terms causes temporal leakage by training on future-term records, creating unrealistic predictive patterns and invalidating prospective generalization testing.
Using the test set in the confirmatory fallback/model-selection step makes final configuration dependent on test outcomes, so the held-out test set is no longer an unbiased generalization estimate.

Flattening the 12x5 weekly sequence into tabular features for gradient boosting removes temporal ordering, so the comparison is about handcrafted snapshot features rather than genuine sequential signal.

Course-level dropout rate computed from a random subset or multi-term training data may leak outcome statistics or be dependent on the temporal slice, giving the aggregate model a shortcut and making the control feature non-stationary.
Recall at FPR <= 0.15 is sensitive to calibration and threshold-selection differences, which can confound metric comparison between models.
Preprocessing artifacts are fit on the full corpus, leaking validation/test information into training and inflating performance.
The Transformer is hyperparameter-tuned while logistic regression is left with a fixed regularization setting, creating an unfair comparison.
Random stratified splitting across all 24 months mixes future and past windows, causing temporal leakage and evaluating interpolation rather than true future deployment.
A gradient-boosted tree baseline is needed in addition to logistic regression to rule out nonlinear tabular signal as the source of any performance gap.

Asymmetric hyperparameter tuning: the multimodal/hypothesis model is fully tuned while the baseline uses a fixed configuration, so any gain may come from extra optimization rather than added features.
Fixed PubMedBERT embeddings may be mismatched to clinical discharge summaries and could miss signal because the encoder was pretrained on biomedical literature rather than note text.
Network-internal readmission matching likely creates systematic false negatives for out-of-network readmissions, with bias correlated with socioeconomic status, mobility, or care loyalty.
Precision at a fixed 80% recall lacks robustness under distribution shift and is not accompanied by pre-specified confidence intervals or power analysis.
The assumption that abstract-domain PubMedBERT geometry separates high-risk from low-risk discharge notes across heterogeneous hospital writing styles is untested.
Checking only note length is insufficient because embedding L2 norm may proxy verbosity and absorb length-related signal.
Out-of-network readmissions treated as non-events introduce non-random label noise correlated with discharge disposition and demographics.
Fixed pre-trained embeddings may leak site identity via institutional style and documentation templates correlated with readmission base rates.
Readmission labels captured only within the hospital network induce structured missingness correlated with patient mobility and socioeconomic factors.
The 80% recall metric is ambiguous because if the threshold is chosen on test labels it leaks information, and if frozen from validation the achieved test recall may differ.
Cohort-mixed splitting leaks temporal information by intermingling overlapping historical periods, so validation is not a true prospective holdout.
The logistic regression baseline omits LMS/activity history features that could be aggregated statically, so the comparison confounds richer inputs with sequence modeling.
First-year retention examples have sequences that are too short for transformer temporal attention to matter, making the sequence-modeling comparison weak.
The split may allow the same student to appear in both train and test across cohorts.
COVID-era behavioral distribution shift may favor the transformer over the static baseline and is not isolated by the cohort-stratified test design.
Calling the logistic regression baseline 'current institutional practice' may overstate the practical uplift if real systems use richer signals.
Earlier-term outcomes from the same student could appear as features in later-term test examples, creating leakage under cohort-mixed splits.
AUC-ROC is the wrong primary metric because operational outreach depends on precision-recall tradeoffs and early-ranked performance; AUPRC would be more appropriate.
Asymmetric hyperparameter tuning budget: transformer gets a full search while logistic regression uses fixed regularization.
Using medication administration inputs that are also part of the Sepsis-3/SOFA label creates treatment-as-label feedback and retrospective leakage.
The time-gap embedding ablation mixes temporal recency encoding with missingness-as-severity signals, making the causal attribution of gains unclear.
Direct overlap between SOFA-component features and inputs means the model may predict organ dysfunction rather than infection-specific sepsis signal.
Bootstrap confidence intervals computed over correlated within-admission windows may underestimate variance and be too narrow.
The missingness sensitivity interpretation is reversed; gains in high-missingness admissions would implicate feature engineering rather than architecture.
SOFA label anchoring may use measurements from within the 6-hour prediction horizon, causing forward leakage into the supervision target.
Window-level calibration metrics such as Brier score and calibration slope may be optimistically biased because within-admission correlation is not fully accounted for.
Time-gap embeddings may encode site-specific lab-ordering or documentation behavior rather than physiology, threatening cross-site generalization.
Non-chronological splitting across months allows future information to influence training and invalidates forward-looking evaluation.
Using corpus-wide summary statistics for preprocessing leaks held-out distributional information into training.
AUROC as the primary metric in a low-prevalence sepsis task hides poor precision/recall trade-offs for early warning.
Temporal leakage from using a random split across the full 24-month window, allowing training on later enrollments and testing on earlier ones.
The inactivity label is structurally entangled with the 14-day observation window / trailing gap, letting the sequence model exploit boundary silence or the trailing inter-event gap as label leakage.

The sequence model has an informational advantage because it uses a 14-day event sequence while the baseline uses 4-week aggregated counts, making the comparison confound temporal structure with horizon length and aggregation.
Truncating sequences at 50 events may disadvantage the highest-engagement learners and reduce the LSTM's discriminative power relative to the baseline.
Padding the 14-day window may encode silence or sequence length in a way that leaks the dropout label.
The baseline omits course ID while the LSTM may learn course-specific behavioral fingerprints, creating an asymmetry in available features.
The capacity gap between an LSTM/Transformer and logistic regression means performance differences cannot be attributed specifically to temporal ordering.
The minimum-activity inclusion criterion excludes easy-to-predict dropouts, potentially reducing the observable advantage of temporal features.
Stratifying by course ID and term does not address platform-level distribution shifts such as UI redesigns or app updates over time.
Using ROC-AUC as the primary metric is misaligned with limited intervention capacity and does not prioritize high-precision ranking of at-risk learners.
Random stratified sampling across time can leak future-period information and violate temporal ordering, overstating real deployment performance.
The ablation removing temporal deltas/timestamps does not isolate temporal dependency learning from sequential co-occurrence or event-pattern matching.
Padding without confirmed masking may let the LSTM use sequence length as a proxy feature, creating a misleading advantage.
Random stratification may split windows from the same customer across train and test, causing customer-level leakage and inflated performance.
The LSTM's larger hyperparameter search space and missing intermediate baseline can bias model comparison through uneven tuning luck.
The label may partly capture natural purchase cadence, and using inter-event time deltas could let the LSTM predict cadence rather than true churn risk.
Fixing sequence length at the 95th percentile may truncate active users and remove informative early-window signals, disadvantaging the LSTM.
Subgroup ROC-AUC comparisons across groups with different prevalence may not be directly commensurable and can weaken subgroup conclusions.
Random stratified splitting of customer observation windows violates temporal dependencies and can leak future-period information into training/testing.
Customer-level random splitting allows the model to memorize individual behavioral fingerprints across train/test windows, inflating apparent performance.
The aggregate baseline omits trend features over sub-windows, so it may miss simple arithmetic signals that the LSTM captures without needing sequence modeling.
The ablation removing time features tests absolute timing effects rather than whether event order matters; a permutation-invariant comparison would be needed.
Selecting models by validation ROC-AUC may not match performance at the operational decision threshold or fixed outreach budget.
The 30-day post-window label may confound natural purchase cadence differences with churn, so the model could be predicting cadence rather than retention.
Sequence truncation direction is unspecified, which could remove recency information for high-activity customers and bias comparisons.
The claim that ordering adds value beyond counts and recency is not directly tested by the proposed ablations.
A strict temporal holdout is needed because random splits can let the model learn period-specific signatures rather than true temporal dependencies.

The temporal-structure ablation cannot separate sequential-order gains from event-feature granularity gains because the LSTM still sees event-type sequences while the baseline logistic regression does not.
PR-AUC or calibrated metrics at the operating point would be more relevant than ROC-AUC for a fixed high-recall deployment threshold.
Baseline conflates feature-set changes with model-class changes, so gains from sequence features cannot be isolated without an RFM-only boosted-tree ablation.
Excluding dormant non-renewers from the positive class/labeling creates selection bias and may inflate apparent performance on the evaluated population.
Non-temporal 5-fold cross-validation within the training period can introduce mild temporal leakage and optimistic hyperparameter selection.
Cohort churn propensity may leak label information if computed non-causally using data beyond each row's observation cutoff.
Segment-specific AUC on irregular-cadence customers is exploratory or potentially circular if the subgroup definition is not pre-registered or reuses model features.
Short-lookback sequence features may be exploiting proximity to the label window rather than capturing genuine behavioral dynamics.
Using the same validation window for both hyperparameter tuning and model selection may overfit to that period and understate uncertainty across alternative windows.
Using AUC-ROC as the primary metric misaligns evaluation with the business objective of prioritizing the top fixed-volume risk segment, where precision/ranking in the high-risk tail matters more.
Labels may be constructed with inconsistent predictive horizons because the horizon is measured from run start rather than the prediction window cutoff.
Positive labels may be tied to work-order proximity or the failure window in a way that introduces administrative or soft look-ahead leakage.

The sequence model is compared against baselines using a richer feature set, while a flattened rich-feature baseline is missing, so gains may reflect feature engineering rather than temporal modeling.
Thresholding at 80% validation recall may produce an unstable operating point that does not transfer reliably to the test set under temporal distribution shift.
Very small numbers of pumps and positive events make stratified analyses, temporal cross-validation, and per-unit generalization tests underpowered.
Attention weights are not faithful explanations, so using them to argue against temporal pattern exploitation is methodologically unsound.
ROC AUC is the wrong primary metric under extreme class imbalance because it does not reflect the operational cost of false positives and low precision.
PR AUC should be reported as a co-primary metric instead of relying on ROC AUC alone under low prevalence.
There may be label noise around the run/label boundary, including near-miss negatives and asymmetric mislabeling of failures just outside the horizon.
Random window-level splitting from the same pumps causes temporal leakage and adjacent degradation trajectories to appear in both train and test.
Using a bidirectional LSTM for deployment introduces non-causal dependence on future timesteps and a long prediction latency.
A 604,800-step LSTM input is computationally infeasible / implausible and likely implies undocumented downsampling or a mismatched raw-sequence baseline.
Optimizing the F1 threshold on a small rare-event validation set is unstable and may not generalize to the test set.
Excluding windows within ±3 days of scheduled maintenance biases the data by removing near-failure events.
The test set is too small because it only contains 28% of the data.
Temporal leakage from window-level random splitting lets adjacent windows from the same pump/degradation episode appear in both train and test.
Using a 604,800-step BiLSTM is computationally implausible without some undocumented truncation/downsampling/chunking, so the model may not be operating on truly raw sequences.
Selecting the F1 threshold on a small validation set with rare positives can yield unstable thresholds and poor transfer to test due to prevalence/nonstationarity differences.
The baseline uses extra maintenance/population-history features such as prior failure counts and days since last maintenance that the sequence model does not have access to.
Random stratified split across the full time span causes temporal leakage, including future information in training and in lookback windows.
MAPE is an inappropriate primary metric for intermittent/low-volume demand and can disproportionately weight small-volume SKUs.
The XGBoost baseline is not tuned with comparable hyperparameter effort to the proposed sequence model, making the comparison asymmetric.
Stockout censoring is not corrected in training labels, and the sequence model's lookback may propagate censored signals more than XGBoost.
Preprocessing transformers are fitted on the full dataset before splitting, leaking validation and test distributional information into training.
The sequence model is tuned on validation while XGBoost is fixed, conflating architectural differences with search budget.
MAPE is used as the tuning metric for the sequence model despite pathologies on low-volume intermittent demand, creating a metric-architecture mismatch.
The experiment does not verify that meaningful temporal autocorrelation exists within the 28-day lookback window for most series.
Using demand[t-365] as a direct feature means the claimed improvement beyond seasonality is not established without an ablation removing that lag feature.
Aggregate RMSE is dominated by high-volume stations, so the headline result may mainly reflect performance on a small subset of large stations rather than network-wide improvement.

Retraining the final model on train+validation while keeping hyperparameters tuned on a smaller split may yield a suboptimally regularized or miscalibrated model because the best settings can change with dataset size.
New stations lacking demand[t-365] gives the SNaive baseline a weaker fallback while the GBT retains other features, creating an asymmetric comparison that may inflate the apparent win.
A curated event schedule may have been assembled with hindsight or knowledge of demand anomalies, which could leak target-correlated information into the event feature.
Comparing only against a weather-blind SNaive baseline is asymmetric on surge or weather-shift days; a weather-aware linear baseline is missing.
Using RMSE alone can hide poorer performance on low-volume or new stations even if aggregate error improves, which matters for operational deployment.
Walk-forward lag computation may have leaked future information if rolling means were precomputed on the full dataset before splitting.
The t-365 lag and SNaive both align on calendar day minus 365, which can misalign weekday effects in leap-year cycles for commuter stations with strong weekday/weekend demand patterns.
Repository-level and temporal splitting constraints may be logically incompatible or under-specified, creating leakage or selection bias depending on implementation.
Pre-trained embeddings may carry external knowledge beyond the training cutoff, so fitting on the training set alone does not fully prevent temporal leakage.
AUPRC on a small, stratified held-out repository set may be unstable and sensitive to repository prevalence/composition rather than model quality.
The baseline includes author defect history, which may overlap with signal captured by the text model and weakens mechanistic attribution claims.
Commit message embeddings may exploit lexical shortcuts such as hotfix/revert terminology instead of semantic defect-related meaning.
AUPRC should specify aggregation and can be misleading when class prevalence differs across train/test repositories or time periods.
PRs near the dataset cutoff may have truncated 180-day observation windows, causing systematic under-labeling of defects in the test set.
It is unclear whether training-period PRs from repositories designated for the test split are included or discarded, which affects training diversity and potential leakage.
Text embeddings and code-change statistics are bundled together without ablation, so the contribution of text-specific signal cannot be isolated.
Sender domain reputation / malicious-message ratio is computed using the full 18-month window, leaking future validation/test label information into training/evaluation features.
The temporal cross-validation / hyperparameter tuning setup is internally inconsistent and may not align with the evaluation regime.
Campaign clustering used for split integrity is not validated, so misclustering could let campaign structure bleed across folds.
The transformer’s advantage may partly come from pretrained weights / domain prior knowledge rather than architecture alone; no randomly initialized encoder baseline is provided.
Equal wall-clock tuning budget gives the GBDT and transformer an asymmetric search opportunity, favoring one model’s trial count.
The positive class prevalence is not stated, making AUPRC values hard to interpret.
Test-set peeking/model selection: using test performance to choose between models contaminates the final evaluation and biases reported performance.
Shuffled-order ablation is needed to show whether temporal order, rather than just the presence of sequence information, drives the sequence model's gain.
Retraining on train+validation with early stopping from a held-out slice of validation creates a temporal mismatch between stopping signal and test distribution.
The 14-day sequence model window has higher event-level granularity than the 30-day aggregate baseline, so improvements may reflect richer input detail rather than temporal modeling.
Label resolution lag may differ by scam type across splits, causing the test positives to be unrepresentative of production because fast- and slow-resolving scams are structurally different.
The final retrained model is not identical to the deployed model because early stopping uses a held-out subset of validation during retraining.
Comparing a 14-day sequence model to a 30-day aggregate baseline confounds architecture with recency/coverage differences; an additional 14-day aggregate baseline would be needed.
A bidirectional LSTM or transformer may leak current-transaction information into prior-event encodings if the current transaction is not strictly excluded from the input sequence.
The 90-day label resolution window can bias the test set toward faster-confirmed scams near the end of the period, distorting absolute AUCPR estimates.
Using 10% of validation for early stopping during final retraining introduces slight optimistic bias because early stopping is correlated with hyperparameter selection.
An offline bidirectional LSTM may not match the causal real-time serving architecture, so the AUCPR comparison may not correspond to a deployable system.
Label censoring near the end of the test window may change the confirmed-scam mix relative to training, confounding the final comparison.
Assigned agent / agent history may leak the target because agent assignment can reflect likely resolution category and may not be available at prediction time.
The ablation design removes agent-history together with other metadata, so it cannot isolate the contribution of agent-history versus other shortcut features.
The comparison between logistic regression on text-only and GBT on text+metadata+agent-history conflates model class with feature set, lacking an intermediate baseline.
A temporally shifted test set may have OOV terminology and class-distribution drift, which can depress macro-F1 and complicate interpretation of absolute performance.
Agent-history features are inherently invalid design choices.
Random stratified splitting by host-day/time can leak future or same-incident attack patterns into training, so chronological splits are needed for realistic deployment evaluation.
The bidirectional LSTM uses future context within the observation window, so it is not a causal forward-looking model and may exploit post-compromise information.
No permutation-invariant bag-of-events baseline is included, making it unclear whether gains come from temporal ordering or from richer per-event features.
±1-day label/window imprecision can contaminate early-detection evaluation by shifting pre-window attack activity into negative labels or near-boundary artifacts.
Selecting models by ROC-AUC under severe class imbalance may favor majority-class ranking over performance at operationally relevant thresholds.

The shuffling ablation does not isolate temporal order because learned embeddings may still encode sequential structure, and/or feature-rich sequence representations are not controlled against the 75-feature baseline.

Random stratified host-time splitting across the full time span causes temporal leakage and shared-history contamination between train and test, including post-incident artifacts and forward-looking label leakage.
Centrality features used by the tabular baseline are computed from the full graph / global partition, leaking future graph topology into the baseline and confounding the graph-vs-tabular comparison.
The 7-day forward labeling window uses future information and may rely on post-hoc forensic attribution not available in real time.
Incident-level AUC is underspecified because the negative set construction and multi-host incident aggregation method are not defined, making the metric potentially misleading.
Per-incident-type AUC for lateral movement may be based on too few positive incidents for reliable confidence intervals or model comparison.

The assumption that lateral movement necessarily produces a distinguishable multi-hop graph signature in 7-day windows is not empirically verified before drawing conclusions about graph-learning advantages.
Bidirectional temporal leakage arises because (host, time_window) examples from later dates can influence earlier test examples through shared neighborhoods and graph embeddings.
If incident-level AUC uses a max-over-hosts rule or globally sampled negatives, the metric may be inflated or artificially easy compared with production conditions.
The 7-day sliding graph window with daily snapshots may be too coarse to capture attacks that unfold within hours, effectively reducing the temporal GNN to a static model.
Tabular baseline uses analyst-response activity counts that leak post-label SOC workflow information tied to the incident
Retrospectively anchoring labels to incident start times creates leakage/circularity and privileges the training/evaluation window
Using test-set performance to choose among tied configurations or make deployment/threshold decisions contaminates the hold-out test set
AUROC is an imperfect primary metric for an operational recall/lead-time objective
The fixed >=0.03 threshold lacks statistical justification and may be within noise
Bootstrap confidence intervals may understate variance because positive incidents are clustered by campaign
The novel-entity hold-out does not isolate temporal drift / infrastructure drift in event-sequence statistics
The secondary hold-out cohort is too small and same-organization entities are not truly out-of-distribution
The success criterion should require both AUROC improvement and positive lead-time advantage
Prior incident-history aggregate features create an entity-reputation / survivorship signal that may favor the model and reflect detection policy rather than genuine telemetry.
The assumption that uninvestigated alerts are benign introduces asymmetric label noise and may systematically mislabel low-priority or telemetry-rich cases.
Stratifying by severity within a temporal split can leak future class distribution information and obscure real drift.
The mapping from ordinal labels to nDCG gains is unspecified, so model ranking could change under different gain schemes.
Prior-incident aggregate features may be denser in retrospective training than at deployment, creating an optimism bias for the proposed feature set.
Analyst ticket notes may contain post-triage label proxies or dispositions unavailable at inference time, letting the model exploit downstream human input.
Using analyst severity tiers as nDCG gains may reduce metric independence because the gains partly reflect the same signals captured by the model features.
nDCG@20 may not align with asymmetric operational costs, so optimizing it could diverge from the stated goal of catching critical incidents.
A single 3-month test window is too short to distinguish generalizable signal from campaign-specific memorization.
Asymmetric hyperparameter tuning: TGNN extensively tuned while tabular baseline is fixed/under-tuned, confounding architectural comparison with optimization effort.
Use of user/cohort splits instead of chronological splits causes temporal leakage and prevents valid forward-looking prediction claims.
Shared infrastructure node embeddings (IPs/endpoints) across cohorts can leak information between user groups, giving the graph model cross-cohort access not available to the baseline.
The TGNN and tabular baseline use different feature representations/aggregation schemes, so observed gains cannot be attributed specifically to graph topology.
Treating unlabeled sessions as negatives unfairly penalizes early-detection alerts before the label window and biases evaluation against the TGNN.
A test-set confirmation step during model selection contaminates the final holdout and invalidates bootstrap confidence intervals as an unbiased estimate.
A single user-cohort split with sparse positives may make results depend on split luck, and bootstrap confidence intervals do not capture split-selection variance.
The experiment does not establish that positive-class sessions have graph neighborhoods distinguishable beyond what tabular aggregates already capture.

Cross-sectional user cohort splits do not test temporal generalization; incident clustering in certain calendar windows could make both models learn campaign-specific patterns instead of general behavior.
Ticket workflow state / early analyst notes may leak disposition or label information because they reflect analyst decisions made during the confirmation process rather than true alert-time features.
Evaluating only reviewed alerts excludes unreviewed alerts and makes the test set non-representative of the full alert stream, so results may not generalize to production ranking utility.

Tuning the LTR model while leaving the baseline fixed/untuned makes the comparison unfair because gains may reflect unequal hyperparameter optimization effort rather than the ranking objective itself.
AUC-PR may be misaligned with the operational top-k objective, so a model can look good on AUC-PR while failing to prioritize the most important alerts in the top-ranked set.
Running a confirmation pass on the test set and restricting candidates adaptively can cause adaptive overfitting to test distribution characteristics.
Survivorship bias / label-set selection bias from analyst review being conditioned on the rule-score ranking, so true positives hidden lower in the ranking are underrepresented in labels.
The baseline is too simplistic because it uses only the rule score, while real analyst triage may incorporate additional heuristics.
Incident-response history features may leak information if disposition timestamps are retroactively updated after post-incident review.
NDCG may be a poor fit for the operational goal because it emphasizes overall ranking rather than top-k performance under analyst capacity limits.
Unreviewed alerts in the test set likely receive a default relevance grade of 0 in NDCG, which can make the metric comparison misleading.
Escalation-rate history features create a circular dependency with analyst labels because they encode prior labeling behavior.
Averaging NDCG across shifts of different sizes can obscure operational differences between easy low-volume and hard high-volume shifts.
The comparison baseline is just a single-feature rule-score ranker, so a simpler linear heuristic baseline is missing.
Training labels are survivorship-biased because review depends on rule severity, so label distribution reflects the baseline's prioritization rather than ground truth severity.
NDCG against analyst dispositions conflates model quality with label quality because the labels are shaped by the triage order.
Incident-response history features assume escalation-rate stationarity across the temporal split, which may be violated by drift.
A full-feature point-wise classifier is missing as a comparison, so gains cannot be attributed specifically to learning-to-rank.
Preprocessing statistics are fit on the full dataset, leaking validation/test information into training and evaluation.
Random student-level splitting across all terms mixes future and past cohorts, causing temporal leakage and unrealistic deployment evaluation.
Using ECE as the sole model-selection or primary evaluation criterion can incentivize misaligned or degenerate calibration-focused solutions.
The logistic regression/static-feature baseline is not hyperparameter-tuned, creating an asymmetric comparison versus the tuned transformer.
The falsification check is underpowered and does not adequately test the intended course-confound hypothesis.
Asset-stratified random window splitting across the full time span causes temporal leakage by mixing future and past windows from the same assets, violating prospective deployment assumptions.
The baseline is not truly static because it includes rolling/rate-change/spectral temporal features, so the comparison is between learned temporal modeling and engineered temporal features.
The reported AUROC comparison lacks confidence intervals or significance testing, so the observed gap may be sampling noise given the small number of positives.
Global AUROC across heterogeneous assets may be dominated by a few high-failure assets and mix inter-asset with within-asset ranking performance.
The feature comparison may be unfair because spectral features might be available to one baseline in a richer form than to the other, confounding temporal modeling with feature access.
The maintenance-schedule confound check is not executable as described because it relies on first-half asset history that is already inside the training set under the current split.
The 7-day feature window ending at t and label window beginning at t creates a boundary-effect / near-instantaneous leakage issue for true 7-day-ahead prediction.
Using AUROC as the primary metric and tuning criterion is inappropriate for highly imbalanced maintenance prediction because it can hide poor precision in the rare-positive operating regime.
Random account-month splitting leaks the same account across train/test, enabling within-account identity memorization and inflating transformer performance.
Using random stratified splitting rather than a strict temporal holdout allows future periods to influence training and violates deployment-time chronology.
Using the test set during development for model stability checks compromises the independence of final evaluation.
Truncating concatenated ticket text to 512 tokens may discard important information for high-volume accounts, especially if the lost content contains the strongest churn signals.
Excluding semantic proxy features from the baseline makes the comparison less informative about whether the transformer is the best overall approach.
Restricting to resolved tickets may introduce selection bias if resolution status depends on information after the prediction date.
Undocumented concatenation order before truncation can bias which text segments the transformer sees.
Comparing a transformer with semantic text inputs to a baseline with only volume features confounds architecture differences with feature-set differences.
Lack of lightweight NLP ablations such as TF-IDF or sentiment features prevents determining whether any gain comes from contextual understanding versus generic text signal.
Chronological concatenation plus 512-token truncation may remove the most recent, highest-signal escalation language for high-volume accounts.
Random stratified splitting creates no strict temporal holdout, so the model may exploit period-specific vocabulary rather than general semantic patterns.
Baseline comparison is confounded because the sequence model also has access to richer historical encounter information, so gains may reflect more data rather than temporal ordering.
The study lacks an ablation isolating sequence/order effects from static feature richness, such as a bag-of-encounters or MLP baseline.
The baseline comparison is confounded because the GRU+FC head can model nonlinear static interactions that logistic regression cannot, so an MLP baseline is needed.
Retraining on train+val after hyperparameter selection may cause a train/validation regime mismatch or validation overfitting, making the final model selection procedure underspecified.
Planned readmission exclusion based on discharge flags, admission reasons, or documentation codes is inconsistently applied across hospitals and patient subgroups, inducing correlated label noise.
AUROC is an inappropriate primary metric for a rare outcome with limited intervention capacity because it ignores precision-recall tradeoffs and high-risk operating points.
Using AUROC both for model selection and final reporting is insufficient because performance in the high-risk tail may differ under AUPRC or precision@recall.
The validation strategy allows future-era coding practices and readmission patterns to influence training, creating temporal leakage and overestimated performance.
The claim that retraining on train+val invalidates the hyperparameter regime because those properties were never validated is not a substantive methodological flaw as stated.
The concern that AUROC gains concentrated at easier sites do not prove generalizable sequence signal is a real external-validity concern, but it is not one of the planted issues.

Baseline logistic regression is under-optimized relative to the proposed model, because it uses fixed regularization / no grid search while the proposed model is tuned, making the comparison ambiguous and uninterpretable.

Merchant-category co-occurrence statistics used to initialize embeddings are computed from the full corpus including validation/test-period data, leaking future information into the feature representation.
The comparison conflates model family and feature set because no ablation evaluates a tuned gradient-boosted model on baseline features alone, so the effect of sequence embeddings cannot be isolated.

The churn label is partly a near-deterministic function of recency, which is already included as a feature, creating a definitional shortcut that may inflate precision@5% and overstate actionable early-intervention value.

Gradient-boosted trees require fixed-width inputs, so raw transaction sequences must be aggregated before modeling; since the aggregation choice is unspecified, it is unclear whether sequence-specific structure is actually being tested.
Precision at top 5% / early stopping may be sensitive to calibration differences between model families and could favor boosted ensembles for reasons unrelated to predictive quality.

The primary metric precision at top 5% is sensitive to base-rate shifts across temporal windows, and without checking churn-rate stability across splits, performance differences may reflect distributional drift rather than model quality.
Incident labels are tied to SOC confirmation time rather than compromise onset, creating label lag that may reflect investigation timing instead of attack timing.

User-disjoint splitting combined with month stratification may concentrate high-activity or later-period users in training, creating temporal leakage / future-period data in training and making evaluation overly optimistic.
Adjusting the threshold after inspecting the test set contaminates the holdout and invalidates Precision@FPR=5% as an unbiased test estimate.
Using absolute event-index positional encoding without elapsed-time information prevents the transformer from modeling temporal spacing in event sequences.
Padding sparse user histories to a fixed length may cause the transformer to rely on coarse event-type counts rather than meaningful sequence structure.
Reporting Precision@FPR=5% without verifying the actual empirical FPR could mean models are compared at different operating points due to calibration differences.
90-day feature lookback may overlap prior renewal cycles for short-contract accounts, leaking prior renewal outcomes into features and inflating performance for that segment.
Temporal train/test split induces a tenure-distribution shift under company growth, making learned tenure effects out of distribution at test time.
Censoring is not independent for long-contract enterprise accounts because they are mechanically censored due to contract duration, violating survival-model assumptions for that segment.
The baseline conflates observation-window length, feature representation, and model class, so performance gains cannot be attributed to temporal structure alone.
Feature engineering before the temporal split may leak population-level statistics if any features are computed over the full dataset instead of training data only.
Using a 30-day baseline window versus a 90-day proposed window confounds longer lookback with temporal modeling, making the comparison unidentifiable.
Evaluating time-dependent AUC at 90 days may be circular if 90 days is the modal renewal date, making the decision horizon poorly chosen.
The comparison mixes temporal-feature benefits with model-class benefits because there is no static-feature model-class-controlled baseline.
Time-dependent AUC and Brier score may be biased if IPCW censoring weights are estimated under a shifted censoring distribution across the temporal split.
Preprocessing statistics were fit on the full cohort, leaking validation/test distribution information into scaling/encoding.
Random user-level splitting across the whole time span mixes future and past data, hiding temporal distribution shift.
The proposed model received hyperparameter tuning while the baseline Cox model used defaults, making the comparison unfair.
The Weibull parametric form may be misspecified because its monotone hazard cannot represent a truly non-monotone risk pattern.
The 90-day history requirement excludes newer users / early churners, biasing the cohort toward easier cases with less challenging behavior.
The fixed months 1–12 feature window creates ambiguous sequence representations for partially observed users, especially under the eligibility filter.
Using time-varying behavioral covariates in a proportional hazards model may violate the proportional hazards assumption and miscalibrate survival curves.
The metric choice (integrated Brier score) emphasizes calibration rather than ranking, which may be misaligned with identifying high-risk users for retention campaigns.
Preprocessing statistics/transformers are fit on the full dataset before splitting, leaking validation/test distribution information into training.
User-level split is random across all calendar periods, so training can contain future data relative to test and evaluation is not truly out-of-time.
The baseline Cox model is left untuned while the proposed model receives cross-validated hyperparameter selection, making the comparison unfair.
Restricting the cohort to users with at least 90 days of history excludes newer subscribers and biases evaluation toward an easier subset.
The prediction anchor is not fixed to a calendar date, so if it varies with outcome it may leak label information into the sequence features.
The Weibull parametric hazard imposes a monotone hazard shape and may be misspecified for non-monotone churn dynamics, harming IBS.
The 0.05 absolute IBS success threshold is arbitrary because it is not anchored to the baseline or null IBS.
Preprocessing transformers are fit on the full cohort before splitting, causing leakage from validation/test into training feature construction.
The test set is used to break ties / select between competing models before final reporting, so it is not a truly untouched hold-out set and reported uncertainty is optimistic.
Clinical BERT embeddings may leak information if embeddings or postprocessing are fit on the full cohort, but this is not clearly established from the described setup.
The main comparison confounds feature set with model class because one arm uses logistic regression and the other uses a GBT/GBM; a same-model-class baseline-only control is missing.
AUROC alone may be insufficient at low prevalence because gains could occur mainly in clinically unimportant regions; a sensitivity-at-threshold criterion is missing.
Clinical BERT note embeddings may still be confounded by documentation intensity / note length / service complexity despite stratification checks.
The baseline model may be under-engineered relative to prior readmission benchmarks, potentially exaggerating the apparent improvement.
Lack of clean-run or multi-seed variance analysis for SPD/EOD makes it unclear whether observed fairness changes exceed natural randomness on small datasets.

The paper does not establish that the anchoring attack specifically places poisoned points in demographically asymmetric or boundary-relevant regions; the fairness effect may just be incidental accuracy degradation.
The influence-function-based attack may rely on an approximation that is not validated for the small datasets/classifiers used, so the optimization proxy may be miscalibrated.
The attack results are not benchmarked against poisoning-detection or sanitization defenses, so reported effectiveness may not survive standard preprocessing defenses.
COMPAS may be an unusually easy target because of its structural properties, limiting generality of the findings.
The influence attack optimizes covariance with the sensitive attribute, but SPD/EOD are discrete fairness metrics; the proxy objective may not align with the reported evaluation.
No comparison is provided against a generic accuracy-targeting poisoning baseline, so it is unclear whether the fairness-specific objective adds anything beyond ordinary label-noise damage.
The non-random anchoring variant may collapse to random anchoring if subgroup membership is ambiguous, and the paper does not include an ablation to detect this failure mode.
There is no baseline of random label-flip poisoning at matched poisoning rates, so the contribution of the fairness-targeted objective cannot be isolated from generic noise injection.
SPD and EOD are reported only as point estimates without confidence intervals or averaging over runs, leaving uncertainty about whether changes exceed random variation.
The attacks do not guarantee simultaneous degradation of both SPD and EOD, so the reporting may be selectively emphasizing whichever metric is affected.
The anchoring attack assumes protected-group members are near the decision boundary, but this precondition is not verified.
The influence attack depends on a well-conditioned Hessian approximation that may fail for the classifiers used, weakening the claimed mechanism.
The covariance objective is only a proxy and is not monotonically tied to SPD or EOD, so maximizing it may not reliably worsen the reported fairness metrics.
The datasets are small tabular benchmarks with pre-existing label quality issues, and the poisoning rates may be unrealistically high to yield measurable fairness degradation.
